title: Usage of the 60s 70s songs in 2xx
wikitext:
[]:             Quote Post by Sailor Star Dust » Thu Apr 14, 2011 3:07 pm Looking at Funimation's translations of those two background songs in 2.22 (the 60s/70s songs. The song that plays in Shinji's room and the song that plays at the bar.), I feel that it's another example (See NGE/EoE itself) of background dialogue/music being rather telling of the current situation in-scene. "Don't turn around" by The Peanuts http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=5ZLBOLLqJD4 have lyrics of the following, And it plays when Shinji's in his room wondering about the dinner party. He wishes that Gendo would be able to attend (little does he know, Gendo was thinking about it til shit got real). "Don't turn around. Don't turn around. I'm begging you. Because, see, I'm fixing my socks right now."

[]:             Quote Post by Warren Peace » Thu Apr 14, 2011 3:11 pm "Season of Love" is great. Glad they put it on the OST, even though it's background. Seems like something Tarantino would use in Kill Bill.

[]:             Quote Post by Sailor Star Dust » Thu Apr 14, 2011 3:35 pm That reminds me, while Kyo no Hi Wa Sayonara (Today is the Day for Goodbye) is a children's song, Tsubasa wo Kudasai (Please Give Me Wings) (which might fall into the same category of being a children's song, I forget) seemingly became popular with different versions in the 60s (or 70s perhaps) by the group The Red Birds. Live performance: http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=7in5rpYU584 Album version: http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Irvkg5TNFwc English version (I Would Give You Anything): http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=tVVOVHt3kzE Of course, Today is the Day for Goodbye (Shinji and Asuka probably meeting again in some manner in Q or Final .) and Please Give Me Wings (Eva-01 gaining wings, Shinji and Rei supposedly gaining freedom from pain, returning to childhood tendencies.) fit beautifully in terms of the lyrics and what happens on screen. I think that was discussed in the general music thread somewhere.

[]:             Quote Post by Seele00TextOnly » Thu Apr 14, 2011 3:41 pm ...

[]:             Quote Post by Xard » Thu Apr 14, 2011 3:49 pm Anno doesn't choose this stuff accidentally. It's indeed important. Good thing you digged up actual lyrics so we know the exact meaning :)

[]:             Quote Post by Reichu » Thu Apr 14, 2011 4:00 pm Xard wrote: Anno doesn't choose this stuff accidentally. It's indeed important.

[]:             Quote Post by Xard » Thu Apr 14, 2011 4:07 pm Reichu wrote: Happiness doesn't come to me, 'cause I'm going to it Take a step in one day, three steps in three days Three steps forward, two steps back Life's a one-two punch

[]:             Quote Post by Jayfive » Thu Apr 14, 2011 4:09 pm Sailor Star Dust wrote: English version (I Would Give You Anything): http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=tVVOVHt3kzE=

[]:             Quote Post by Reichu » Thu Apr 14, 2011 4:13 pm Xard wrote: sets up Mari's character and how she relates to piloting eva perfectly, doesn't it?

[]:             Quote Post by FreakyFilmFan4ever » Thu Apr 14, 2011 5:42 pm Reichu wrote: It sets up the whole movie perfectly, IMO.

[]:             Quote Post by Sailor Star Dust » Thu Apr 14, 2011 5:45 pm Can't believe I forgot to mention "Mari's" song. It's called 365 Step March and was originally sung by Kiyoko Suizenji http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=mUe84vi51Aw , for those who didn't know that yet. (My fingers are crossed there will be some Maaya Sakamoto single of it at some point.) Reichu wrote: It sets up the whole movie perfectly, IMO.

[]:             Quote Post by Jayfive » Thu Apr 14, 2011 6:09 pm Lordy, theres a LOT of versions of Tsubasa, be they the english or Japanese versions.

[]:             Quote Post by Kendrix » Fri Apr 15, 2011 8:07 am Jayfive wrote: Lordy, theres a LOT of versions of Tsubasa, be they the english or Japanese versions.

[]:             Quote Post by Sailor Star Dust » Fri Apr 15, 2011 10:04 am Kendrix wrote: apparently, there were some screenings of alternate versions of the final scene with that song, if I got that right.

[]:             Quote Post by Jayfive » Tue Apr 26, 2011 3:55 pm and then, there's the cheesy love song "I would give you anything" that uses the same melody of tsubasa kudasai, and aparently, there were some screenings of alternate versions of the final scene with that song, if I got that right.

[]:             Quote Post by NAveryW » Sat Apr 30, 2011 6:11 pm Kendrix wrote: and then, there's the cheesy love song "I would give you anything" that uses the same melody of tsubasa kudasai, and aparently, there were some screenings of alternate versions of the final scene with that song, if I got that right.

[]:             Quote Post by Hyper Shinchan » Wed Jun 29, 2011 8:44 pm Wow, I didn't know that Tsubasa wo Kudasai was such an old song, it's my favourite song from Rebuild 2.0 so I've tried to search for additional infos and the French wikipedia contains something about it (the only other versions of the page are the Japanese and Korean ones), I'm going to translate them as best I can (obviously any correction would be appreciated, neither English nor French is my native language): Tsubasa wo kudasai (翼をください) is a song written by Michio Yamagami (山上路夫) and composed by Kunihiko Murai (村井邦彦). The song apperead in the single Takeda no komoriuta (竹田の子守唄) of the folk group Akaitori (赤い鳥) (my note: Akaitori is Red Birds, the group mentioned by SSD before), released on 5 February 1971. Basically the song was composed for the Nemu Popular Festival '70 (合歓ポピュラーフェスティバル'70, nemu popyurâ fesutibaru '70 ) held in Shima in 1970. In September 1973, Sumiko Yamagata released a reprise in the album Anohi no koto ha (あの日のことは). Later Shôji Hajimoto of the editor Kyouiku Geijutsu Sha decided to include it in a textbook so that it was sung by choirs. It became, during the second half of the 70s, the favourite songs of many Japanese. Kaori Kawamura made a reprise in 1991. In November 1997, Junko Yamamoto, the singer of the group Akaitori, released a single of the song. It was chosen as official song of the Japanese football (my note: soccer for you Americans) team during the 1998 FIFA World Cup. In 1972, Akaitori released an English version as well, titled I would give you anything .

[]:             Quote Post by symbv » Wed Jun 29, 2011 9:48 pm Hyper Shinchan wrote: Wow, I didn't know that Tsubasa wo Kudasai was such an old song, it's my favourite song from Rebuild 2.0 so I've tried to search for additional infos

[]:             Quote Post by Hyper Shinchan » Wed Jun 29, 2011 10:32 pm ^This is funny, I've ended up wasting time with my little research because I'm lazy (or maybe simply stupid, I didn't consider that the matter could have been covered already here); anyway thanks for the links.

[]:             Quote Post by TheFriskyIan » Wed Jun 29, 2011 10:47 pm Tsubasa Wo Kudasai was the best

The song that plays in Shinji's room and the song that plays at the bar.), I feel that it's another example (See NGE/EoE itself) of background dialogue/music being rather telling of the current situation in-scene. "Don't turn around"

Article: Usage of the 60s 70s songs in 2xx
 (obviously any correction would be appreciated, neither English nor French is my native language): Tsubasa wo kudasai (翼をください) is a song written by Michio Yamagami (山上路夫) and composed by Kunihiko Murai (村井邦彦). The song apperead in the single Takeda no komoriuta (竹田の子守唄) of the folk group Akaitori (赤い鳥) (my note: Akaitori is Red Birds, the group mentioned by SSD before), released on 5 February 1971. Basically the song was composed for the Nemu Popular Festival '70 (合歓ポピュラーフェスティバル'70, nemu popyurâ fesutibaru '70 ) held in Shima in 1970. In September 1973, Sumiko Yamagata released a reprise in the album Anohi no koto ha (あの日のことは). Later Shôji Hajimoto of the editor Kyouiku Geijutsu Sha decided to include it in a textbook so that it was sung by choirs. It became, during the second half of the 70s, the favourite songs of many Japanese. Kaori Kawamura made a reprise in 1991. In November 1997, Junko Yamamoto, the singer of the group Akaitori, released a single of the song. It was chosen as official song of the Japanese football (my note: soccer for you Americans) team during the 1998 FIFA World Cup. In 1972, Akaitori released an English version as well, titled I would give you anything .
